Title: High-resolution time-series transcriptomic and metabolomic profiling reveals the regulatory mechanism underlying salt tolerance in maize

Common_Genes.Applied_Species_Variety: HLZY; JI853; receptor line KN5585 for transgenics
Common_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Use ZmGLN2 as a target to maintain amino-acid homeostasis and improve salt tolerance in maize. Favor allele mining or stress-inducible upregulation rather than knockout, because loss of function reduced biomass under salt. It may be stacked with osmotic-protection genes such as myo-inositol/proline pathway genes or early-response regulators like ZmGLK44. Monitor Na+/K+ balance and broader nitrogen/carbon metabolism during deployment.
Common_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Positive regulator of maize salt tolerance by sustaining amino acid accumulation, especially under salt stress; mutants show reduced fresh weight and dry mass under salt and reduced accumulation of several amino acids including Pro, Gly, Orn, Met, Ser, Ala, Thr and Glu.
Common_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: EMS loss-of-function mutants (gln2-1, gln2-2); salt tolerance phenotyping; RNA-seq of mutant versus wild type; GC-MS metabolite profiling; predicted gene-metabolite network; ion measurements.
Common_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: 200 mM NaCl salt stress; analyses emphasized ~8 days after salt treatment and broader time series from 0 h to 18 DAS.
Common_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: Among six maize GLN genes, only ZmGLN2 showed salt-induced expression; highlighted by temporal clustering as earlier activated in the tolerant line HLZY.
Common_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Source_Species: Same as applied species; salt-induced in maize shoots.
Common_Genes.Interacting_Proteins: Gly; Gln; Orn; Met (protein-compound interactions supported by STITCH 5)
Common_Genes.Omics_Data: Time-series RNA-seq (GSA: CRA021765); metabolomics/lipidomics and GC-MS metabolomics (OMIX: OMIX011277); mutant RNA-seq and metabolite profiling described in study.
Common_Genes.Other_Important_Info: ZmGLN2 encodes a cytoplasmic glutamine synthetase in the GS/GOGAT cycle, responsible for a major portion of plant nitrogen assimilation. Predicted to regulate Gly, Gln, Orn, BABA, Met and Pro; experimental validation supported most predictions except glutamine content, likely due to rapid turnover.
Common_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Loss of function disrupted carbon/nitrogen balance under salt stress, increasing carbon skeleton metabolites (pyruvate, cis-aconitate, 2-oxoglutarate, erythrose) while decreasing amino acids; under control conditions growth was largely normal.
Common_Genes.Protein_Family_or_Domain: Glutamine synthetase (GS/GLN) family; cytoplasmic GS1-type enzyme.
Common_Genes.Source_Species_Variety: HLZY/JI853; mutants characterized in maize background from EMS bank
Common_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. ZmGLN2 is the only maize GLN family member showing clear salt-induced expression in this study. 2. Loss-of-function mutants reduce salt tolerance and biomass accumulation under salt stress. 3. ZmGLN2 is required to maintain accumulation of multiple amino acids, especially proline-related stress metabolism, under salt stress, linking nitrogen assimilation to salt adaptation.
Common_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite: Amino acids under salt stress, especially proline
Common_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Osmoprotection, stress adaptation, nitrogen storage/transport, maintenance of carbon-nitrogen balance during salt stress.

Common_Genes.Applied_Promoters: pRD101 inducible promoter for overexpression; CRISPR/Cas9 construct driven by maize U6 for gRNAs and ZmUbi-hspCas9 in editing vector
Common_Genes.Applied_Species_Variety: KN5585 transgenic background; HLZY and JI853 in discovery analyses
Common_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: A promising positive target for breeding salt-tolerant maize, especially for improving osmotic-phase tolerance. Overexpression or activation could be combined with metabolic-protection genes such as ZmGLN2 or MIPS genes. Because ion homeostasis was unchanged, it is best paired with ion-exclusion traits for broader salt tolerance.
Common_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Positive regulator of maize salt tolerance; knockout reduces fresh and dry weight under salt, while overexpression increases fresh weight, dry mass and survival under salt. It acts mainly in osmotic response rather than ion-stress control.
Common_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Temporal clustering-based candidate discovery; CRISPR knockout lines; overexpression transgenics; qPCR validation of induced expression; salt phenotyping; ion measurements.
Common_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: 200 mM NaCl salt stress; strongest inferred role in early osmotic phase before major ionic imbalance.
Common_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: Cluster 4 gene with earlier salt-induced activation in tolerant line HLZY (by 5 DAS) and delayed induction in sensitive line JI853 (around 9 DAS).
Common_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Source_Species: Same as applied species; salt-responsive in maize.
Common_Genes.Omics_Data: Time-series RNA-seq (GSA: CRA021765); metabolomics (OMIX: OMIX011277); transgenic and mutant phenotyping in study.
Common_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Previously implicated in drought resistance. In this study, no significant change in Na+, K+, or Na+/K+ ratio was observed in knockout or overexpression lines, supporting a role in osmotic-stress adaptation rather than ion toxicity management.
Common_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: No obvious growth penalty under normal conditions was reported for knockout or overexpression lines in the tested setting; broader field effects unknown.
Common_Genes.Subcellular_Localization: Nucleus (inferred from TF identity; not directly tested here)
Common_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. ZmGLK44 was identified by temporal clustering as an early HLZY-responsive gene. 2. CRISPR knockout impaired salt tolerance, while overexpression improved biomass and survival under salt stress. 3. Its effect is primarily associated with osmotic-stress tolerance rather than Na+/K+ homeostasis.
Common_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite: Stress-protective metabolic state under salt stress
Common_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Class: Not a single terminal metabolite established
Common_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Supports salt adaptation, especially osmotic stress resilience.

Pathway_Genes.Biosynthetic_Pathway: GS/GOGAT cycle; primary nitrogen assimilation; amino acid biosynthesis support
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: A strong engineering target for improving salt tolerance via maintenance of nitrogen assimilation and amino acid pools. Prefer upregulation or favorable alleles, potentially combined with proline-biosynthetic genes or regulators of early salt response. Avoid knockout.
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: ATP-dependent assimilation of ammonium into glutamate to form glutamine in the cytosol, feeding the GS/GOGAT cycle and downstream amino acid biosynthesis.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Promotes accumulation of multiple amino acids under salt stress and enhances maize salt tolerance.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: EMS mutants; salt phenotyping; transcriptomics; GC-MS metabolite profiling; network prediction plus experimental metabolite validation.
Pathway_Genes.End_Product_Connection_Type: Supplies precursor pool for target amino acids and supports stress-protective proline accumulation.
Pathway_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: 200 mM NaCl salt stress; 8 DAS mutant metabolomics emphasized.
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: Salt-induced in maize shoots; the only GLN gene among six family members with clear salt induction in this study.
Pathway_Genes.Metabolic_Step_Position: Upstream/core assimilation step feeding amino acid biosynthesis
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Although glutamine is the direct product, its steady-state level did not significantly change in mutants under salt, likely due to rapid turnover; nevertheless multiple downstream amino acids decreased strongly.
Pathway_Genes.Pathway_Branch_or_Subpathway: Cytosolic GS1 branch of nitrogen assimilation linked to proline/ornithine/glycine/methionine-related metabolism
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Disruption causes carbon-nitrogen imbalance under salt, with elevated carbon skeleton metabolites and reduced amino-acid accumulation.
Pathway_Genes.Rate_Limiting_or_Key_Control_Step: Key control step in nitrogen assimilation under salt stress; central hub, but not explicitly claimed rate-limiting in maize salt tolerance
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. ZmGLN2 encodes a cytoplasmic GS enzyme central to nitrogen assimilation. 2. Salt-induced ZmGLN2 is necessary to sustain multiple amino acids under salt stress. 3. Its disruption lowers proline and other amino acids and reduces salt tolerance.
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Osmoprotection and nitrogen metabolism during salt adaptation.

Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: MIPS genes are promising targets to raise endogenous myo-inositol and improve salt tolerance. They may be stacked with stress regulators or ion-homeostasis genes. Basal accumulation appears advantageous, so promoter editing or allele selection for higher constitutive/stress-inducible expression in tolerant backgrounds may be useful.
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Converts glucose-6-phosphate into myo-inositol-1-phosphate, the first committed/rate-limiting step toward myo-inositol biosynthesis.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Higher expression of specific MIPS genes is associated with higher myo-inositol levels in tolerant maize, and myo-inositol promotes salt tolerance and shoot K+ accumulation.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Time-series transcriptomics identifying salt-induced MIPS genes; metabolite profiling of myo-inositol; exogenous myo-inositol application under salt stress.
Pathway_Genes.End_Product_Connection_Type: Forms committed precursor for target product myo-inositol.
Pathway_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: 200 mM NaCl salt stress; exogenous 50 μM myo-inositol pretreatment before salt.
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: Three salt-induced MIPS genes were identified; ZmMIPS1-1 and ZmMIPS2-2 showed higher expression in HLZY, while ZmMIPS2-1 was more strongly expressed in JI853.
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Paper states MIPS regulates the rate-limited step in myo-inositol biosynthesis. Direct genetic perturbation of individual MIPS genes was not reported in this study.
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Not directly tested genetically; altered inositol metabolism could affect cell wall synthesis and signaling.
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. ZmMIPS1-1 is one of three salt-induced MIPS genes in maize. 2. Its expression is higher in the tolerant line HLZY, consistent with higher basal myo-inositol. 3. MIPS-mediated inositol synthesis is implicated as a favorable metabolic feature for salt tolerance.
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Stress adaptation, cell wall synthesis, signaling, ROS mitigation, support of K+ accumulation.

Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Candidate target for tuning endogenous myo-inositol levels, but line-specific expression suggests paralog selection matters. Best deployed with metabolite validation rather than assuming all MIPS paralogs are equally beneficial.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Salt-induced MIPS paralog associated with myo-inositol biosynthesis; more strongly expressed in JI853 than HLZY despite lower basal myo-inositol in JI853.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Time-series RNA-seq and metabolite profiling; no direct gene-specific mutant validation in this paper.
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: Salt-induced; expressed more strongly in JI853.
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Expression abundance alone did not fully explain tolerant phenotype, suggesting paralog specialization or additional pathway control points.
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Unclear due to lack of direct perturbation.
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. ZmMIPS2-1 is salt induced in maize. 2. It is expressed more strongly in the sensitive line JI853. 3. This indicates that regulation of myo-inositol content is paralog- and context-dependent.

Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Good candidate for increasing beneficial inositol pools in salt-tolerant breeding, particularly where higher expression correlates with tolerant backgrounds. May be combined with ion-homeostasis traits.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Salt-induced MIPS paralog with higher expression in HLZY, consistent with elevated myo-inositol associated with salt tolerance.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Time-series RNA-seq; metabolite profiling; exogenous myo-inositol functional assay at metabolite level.
Pathway_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: 200 mM NaCl salt stress; 50 μM myo-inositol pretreatment evidence supports pathway relevance.
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Part of the evidence linking HLZY’s higher basal myo-inositol to improved salt tolerance.
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. ZmMIPS2-2 is a salt-induced MIPS paralog in maize. 2. It is more highly expressed in the tolerant line HLZY. 3. It likely contributes to advantageous myo-inositol accumulation under salt stress.
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Stress adaptation, signaling, ROS buffering, support of K+ accumulation.

Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Useful target for engineering earlier and stronger proline accumulation during salt stress, potentially together with ZmGLN2 or early-response TFs. Needs careful tuning because proline metabolism is tightly linked to nitrogen status.
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Catalyzes the ATP- and NADPH-dependent conversion of glutamate to glutamate-semialdehyde/Δ1-pyrroline-5-carboxylate, the key committed step in proline biosynthesis.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Expression pattern aligns with earlier proline accumulation in tolerant HLZY compared with JI853 under salt stress.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Correlation of time-series transcriptome with metabolite profiles; mentioned as key enzyme in proline biosynthesis. No direct gene perturbation in this study.
Pathway_Genes.End_Product_Connection_Type: Forms immediate committed precursor for proline.
Pathway_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: 200 mM NaCl salt stress; proline rises earlier in HLZY (3–5 DAS) than in JI853 (5–9 DAS).
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: Salt-responsive; expression pattern matches earlier proline accumulation in HLZY.
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Genes involved in proline metabolism were down-regulated in gln2 mutants, linking nitrogen assimilation to proline pathway output.
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Not directly tested here; over-accumulation of proline pathways can affect nitrogen allocation and growth.
Pathway_Genes.Rate_Limiting_or_Key_Control_Step: Key control/committed step in proline biosynthesis
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. ZmP5CS1 expression tracks salt-induced proline accumulation. 2. Earlier activation in HLZY matches earlier proline rise in the tolerant line. 3. This supports proline biosynthesis as an important metabolic feature of salt adaptation.

Regulation_Genes.Applied_Promoters: pRD101 inducible promoter for overexpression; CRISPR/Cas9 construct using ZmU6 gRNAs and ZmUbi-hspCas9 for gene editing
Regulation_Genes.Applied_Species_Variety: KN5585 transgenic background; HLZY and JI853 for expression discovery
Regulation_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Overexpression/activation of ZmGLK44 is a plausible strategy to improve early osmotic-phase salt tolerance in maize. Best used in stacks with ion-homeostasis genes or metabolic genes such as ZmGLN2/MIPS because it did not alter Na+/K+ balance.
Regulation_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Acts as a positive regulator of maize salt tolerance, improving biomass and survival under salt stress.
Regulation_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Temporal clustering; CRISPR knockout; overexpression transgenics; qPCR; salt phenotyping; ion measurements.
Regulation_Genes.Decisive_Influence_on_Target_Product: Condition-dependent positive controller of salt-adaptive physiological/metabolic state, especially during osmotic stress phase.
Regulation_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: 200 mM NaCl salt stress; effect most evident in osmotic response phase.
Regulation_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: Earlier salt induction in HLZY (by 5 DAS) and delayed induction in JI853 (around 9 DAS); identified in Cluster 4 of earlier HLZY-responsive genes.
Regulation_Genes.Metabolic_Process_Controlled: Salt-stress adaptation linked to osmotic response; likely control of stress-responsive gene expression rather than ion homeostasis.
Regulation_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Previously linked to drought resistance. In this study, no significant effect on Na+, K+, or Na+/K+ ratio was observed, indicating regulatory action outside direct ion exclusion/uptake.
Regulation_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: No obvious normal-growth penalty reported in tested conditions; broader agronomic impacts unknown.
Regulation_Genes.Primary_Regulatory_Targets: Not directly identified in this paper.
Regulation_Genes.Regulation_Mode: Likely transcriptional activation of salt-responsive programs; direct targets not tested.
Regulation_Genes.Regulatory_Effect_on_Target_Genes: Promotes earlier salt-responsive transcriptional program in tolerant maize background; specific direct target genes not resolved.
Regulation_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. ZmGLK44 is an early salt-responsive TF identified by temporal clustering. 2. Knockout compromises salt tolerance whereas overexpression enhances biomass and survival under salt. 3. Its action is associated with osmotic-stress response, not altered Na+/K+ homeostasis.
Regulation_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Class: Trait-level regulation rather than a single metabolite
Regulation_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Supports survival and growth under salt stress.
Regulation_Genes.Upstream_Signals_or_Inputs: Salt stress, especially early osmotic stress.

Regulation_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Candidate regulatory marker for early salt-defense activation in breeding. Could be stacked with metabolic genes, but direct engineering value for nutrient metabolism is not yet established in this paper.
Regulation_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Early upregulation in tolerant HLZY suggests contribution to rapid defense/salt response integration.
Regulation_Genes.Decisive_Influence_on_Target_Product: Putative early salt-defense regulator, but not functionally validated here.
Regulation_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: 1 h after salt stress in HLZY.
Regulation_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: Significantly upregulated in HLZY at 1 h after salt stress; highlighted among defense-related early-response genes.
Regulation_Genes.Metabolic_Process_Controlled: Integration of defense pathways with salt-stress response.
Regulation_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Role inferred partly from Arabidopsis homolog knowledge; no direct maize functional assay in this paper.
Regulation_Genes.Regulatory_Effect_on_Target_Genes: Early activation of defense/salt-responsive genes inferred, but direct targets not established.
Regulation_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. ZmWRKY33 is rapidly induced at 1 h in salt-tolerant HLZY. 2. It marks early coupling of defense and salt signaling. 3. Functional contribution is inferred, not directly tested here.
Regulation_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite: Not assigned to a specific nutrient metabolite

Regulation_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Potential regulator for enhancing early salt/ethylene-defense signaling, but direct metabolic engineering value remains to be validated.
Regulation_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Early induction in tolerant HLZY suggests positive involvement in rapid salt-responsive defense signaling.
Regulation_Genes.Decisive_Influence_on_Target_Product: Putative early regulator of salt-defense signaling; not proven as decisive by functional genetics here.
Regulation_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: 1 h after salt stress in HLZY.
Regulation_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: Upregulated in HLZY at 1 h after salt stress.
Regulation_Genes.Metabolic_Process_Controlled: Ethylene/defense-associated stress response likely linked to salt adaptation.
Regulation_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Evidence in this paper is expression-based and homolog-informed.
Regulation_Genes.Regulatory_Effect_on_Target_Genes: Likely activates ethylene/defense-responsive genes; direct targets not determined here.
Regulation_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. ZmERF1 is an early salt-induced gene in HLZY. 2. It supports the idea that tolerant maize rapidly engages ethylene/defense pathways. 3. Direct functional and target-gene validation was not performed in this study.
Regulation_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite: Not assigned to a specific nutrient metabolite
